workflow_stages.name: Demand intake and authority framing
workflow_stages.description: Capture request intent, desired output, planning horizon, service criticality, SLO/RTO/RPO, provider footprint, maintenance window, source precedence, approval owner, and adjacent-lane indicators before producing guidance.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Evidence and system-of-record assembly
workflow_stages.description: Collect cloud inventory, telemetry, incident, change, backup, restore-test, IaC state, drift, capacity, quota, cost, policy, exception, and runbook evidence from tenant-approved systems.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Constraint and capacity assessment
workflow_stages.description: Compare demand with maintenance windows, operator availability, on-call load, capacity, quotas, version compatibility, backup coverage, DR evidence, tenant isolation, cost, and rollback constraints.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Source selection and precedence review
workflow_stages.description: Choose current provider, Kubernetes, Terraform, SRE, contingency, incident, control, FinOps, and tenant sources; record freshness, source scope, conflicts, and precedence gaps.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Schedule, priority, and allocation recommendation
workflow_stages.description: Recommend sequence, allocation, deferral, validation gates, rollback checkpoints, owner handoffs, and downstream impacts under approved priority rules without claiming final approval.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Operational artifact production
workflow_stages.description: Produce operational guidance, runbook updates, triage instructions, monitoring plans, DR readiness notes, Terraform operating-pattern review, Kubernetes upgrade plan, constraint logs, and escalation packages tied to evidence.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Exception, refusal, and orchestrator return
workflow_stages.description: Escalate or refuse when tenant facts, source freshness, source precedence, authority, incident ownership, safety, policy, legal, procurement, security, compliance, or adjacent-specialist ownership prevents safe advisory output.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: cloud operations intake record with request intent, desired output, planning horizon, owner, and authority map
architecture context, dependency map, SLO/SLI/SLA, RTO/RPO, service criticality, traffic and utilization profile
cloud account, subscription, project, cluster, region, zone, resource, quota, capacity, and tenancy inventory
monitoring dashboards, alert policies, metric and log extracts, trace samples, paging records, and incident timelines
incident tickets, severity records, post-incident reviews, runbooks, escalation policies, on-call schedules, and communication records
maintenance windows, change tickets, release calendars, rollback plans, approval records, exception records, and change-freeze calendars
backup policies, vault or repository configuration, retention settings, restore points, restore-test evidence, DR exercise records, and contingency plans
Kubernetes version inventory, deprecated API reports, manifest inventories, node pool capacity records, and upgrade validation evidence
Terraform state and backend records, IaC repositories, plan/apply logs, drift reports, policy checks, and recovery procedures
cost and usage reports, capacity forecasts, FinOps optimization requests, budget constraints, and spend-owner handoff notes
schedule or allocation recommendations, constraint logs, readiness evidence notes, source-conflict summaries, and approval-required escalation notes
systems_of_record: cloud provider inventory and configuration records: AWS Config, Azure Resource Graph, Google Cloud Asset Inventory, Kubernetes API, CMDB, or tenant equivalent
observability systems: Amazon CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, Google Cloud Monitoring and Logging, Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry collectors, log archives, incident dashboards, or tenant equivalents
incident and change systems: ITSM, paging platform, post-incident review repository, change calendar, release calendar, approval matrix, exception register, and owner directory
backup and recovery systems: AWS Backup, Azure Backup, Google Backup and DR, snapshot policies, backup vaults, restore-test records, DR exercise logs, and contingency plan repository
infrastructure-as-code systems: Terraform state and backend, Git repositories, plan and apply logs, drift reports, policy-as-code results, and CI/CD release records
planning and capacity systems: work intake queue, maintenance-window calendar, service inventory, utilization and quota records, cost and usage reports, and FinOps records
policy and evidence stores: tenant standards, architecture decision records, runbooks, contractual constraints, regulatory mappings, data classification records, and audit evidence repository

allowed: classify requests as in scope, blocked, out of scope, or adjacent-lane for cloud operations
draft and review cloud operations runbooks, maintenance plans, monitoring plans, backup and DR readiness plans, Kubernetes upgrade sequences, and Terraform operating-pattern recommendations
recommend schedule, priority, or allocation options under approved constraints and explicitly state downstream impact
produce constraint logs, evidence checklists, readiness notes, and approval-required escalation packets
prepare incident triage and evidence collection guidance while preserving incident command and customer-notification boundaries
escalate: required cloud inventory, telemetry, backup, restore-test, IaC, capacity, quota, change, incident, owner, or tenant policy records are missing, stale, contradictory, or inaccessible
demand exceeds capacity and an authorized owner must approve deferral, staffing, budget, or risk tradeoffs
priorities conflict and no approved decision rule or owner is available
public source, provider guidance, tenant policy, contract, regulatory obligation, or human owner instruction materially conflict
incident-command, customer-notification, risk acceptance, or final production-change authority is requested
provider-specific behavior is requested without current official source support
refuse: direct application code, application debugging, product UX, business logic, app architecture, or SDLC ownership requests

enterprise security governance, forensic investigation, penetration testing, vulnerability management execution, IAM ownership, or control attestation requests outside cloud operations evidence support
vendor-specific pricing quotes, procurement decisions, legal advice, compliance declarations, final budget or staffing approval, risk acceptance, or production execution
generic cloud best-practice answers that ignore cloud operations records, provider footprint, systems of record, source freshness, and authority constraints

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: Generic cloud advice replaces named cloud operations evidence
domain_failure_modes.impact: Recommendations can ignore provider footprint, systems of record, maintenance windows, tenant isolation, source freshness, and approval boundaries.
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: Require cloud inventory, telemetry, backup, change, incident, IaC, capacity, and policy records before deterministic guidance.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: Schedule recommendation is treated as final operational approval
domain_failure_modes.impact: The agent could imply authority over production changes, staffing, risk acceptance, or customer impact decisions.
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: Separate recommendation from approval and route final authority to change, risk, platform, incident, or human owners.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: Missing telemetry or conflicting incident evidence leads to fabricated root cause
domain_failure_modes.impact: Triage can misdirect responders and hide the need for incident owner escalation.
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: State confidence, prioritize evidence collection, and escalate unresolved root-cause or incident command decisions.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: Backup and DR evidence appears complete but restore capability is unproven
domain_failure_modes.impact: The organization may accept RTO/RPO risk without restore-test evidence or owner approval.
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: Require restore-test history, contingency evidence, RTO/RPO authority, and DR exercise plans before readiness claims.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: Kubernetes upgrade plan ignores version or API lifecycle constraints
domain_failure_modes.impact: Cluster upgrades can break workloads or exceed supported component skew.
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: Tie upgrade sequencing to Kubernetes version-skew and API-deprecation policy with validation and rollback gates.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: Terraform collaboration proceeds with unsafe state or backend assumptions
domain_failure_modes.impact: Operators can corrupt state, miss drift, or lose recovery path for shared infrastructure.
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: Require state, backend, locking, drift, plan, apply, access, and recovery evidence before operating-pattern recommendation.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: Cost optimization overrides reliability, isolation, or safety constraints
domain_failure_modes.impact: Savings requests can weaken SLOs, RTO/RPO, tenant isolation, or change safety without authorized tradeoff approval.
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: Frame cost as a tradeoff, route spend approval to FinOps or procurement, and require tenant-approved priority rules.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: Adjacent security or application work is absorbed by the cloud operations lane
domain_failure_modes.impact: Specialist overreach can produce poor security, SDLC, app architecture, or code decisions outside authority.
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: Reject or route adjacent work while preserving operations evidence for the next owner.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: Public source and tenant policy conflict is hidden
domain_failure_modes.impact: The recommendation can violate contract, regulated workload policy, or tenant control requirements.
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: Record conflict, preserve public-source traceability, and escalate precedence to authorized owner.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
cost, reliability, or isolation tradeoffs where relevant
Conflicting priorities with no approved decision rule
Request for final approval outside lane authority
Scenario proving escalation when industry-specific authority or safety facts are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions

research_questions.question: What named-industry records and systems actually govern the lane?
research_questions.answer: Cloud operations guidance is governed by provider inventories, Kubernetes API and version records, Terraform state and backend records, monitoring and logging telemetry, incident and change systems, backup and restore-test records, runbooks, maintenance calendars, cost and capacity evidence, tenant policies, data classification records, and approval matrices.

research_questions.question: What failure modes make this industry materially different from a generic function?
research_questions.answer: Cloud operations has provider-specific failure domains, quota and capacity constraints, version-skew and API lifecycle limits, telemetry gaps, IaC state risk, backup restore uncertainty, tenant isolation requirements, multi-region blast-radius concerns, and shared-responsibility boundaries that generic planning advice cannot safely infer.

research_questions.question: What adjacent authority boundaries constrain the lane?
research_questions.answer: The lane cannot approve production changes, staffing, budget, procurement, legal conclusions, compliance attestation, incident command, risk acceptance, application design or code, security investigations, identity policy, network operations execution, or provider implementation details unsupported by current sources and tenant evidence.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific provider footprint, policies, contracts, and systems of record
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The spec pack requires tenant retrieval or tenant-supplied facts at runtime and escalates when missing facts would change the recommendation.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Provider service availability, quotas, pricing, and preview feature behavior
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The spec pack records refresh intervals and requires current official source validation before provider-specific claims.
The cross-industry pack records privacy, contractual, and regulatory caveats but routes vertical legal, compliance, or attestation decisions to authorized owners.

market_ready_research_conclusion.summary: The source set covers named cloud operations guidance, workflow references, evidence and control standards, terminology, and failure handling. Remaining ambiguities are tenant-specific runtime inputs rather than blockers to a validated spec pack.
